title: Name the fear. Sit with it.
subtitle: A short meditation for making fear specific and tolerable
excerpt: A ten-minute meditation for taking a diffuse, generalised fear and naming it precisely, sitting with the named version, and finding the named thing smaller and more workable than the unnamed dread.

practice.whenToUse: When fear is running as a diffuse dread — unnamed, unspecified, large — rather than pointing at something precise

content.text: Unnamed fear is generally larger than named fear. The dread that hangs in the background without a specific object is harder to work with than the precise, named thing. This meditation moves from the diffuse to the specific. Then it sits with the specific version to find its actual size.
Sit somewhere quiet. Close your eyes. Take five slow breaths, exhale slower than inhale. Let the body get a little heavier. You are not trying to calm the fear yet. Just arrive.

Ask yourself: what specifically am I afraid of? Not the general version. The precise one. What is the worst thing that you are fearing? Let the answer arrive without editing it. The more specific, the better. Name it in one sentence if you can.
Sit with the named thing (5 minutes)

Hold the named fear and stay with it. Don't try to solve it, argue with it, or reason your way out of it. Just notice: is it as large as the diffuse version was? What does it feel like in the body? Where does it sit?
You can also ask: if this thing happened, what would you do? Not to dismiss the fear. To find out whether the named version is survivable.
When the ten minutes are up, open your eyes. The fear may still be there. But it is a named, specific thing now rather than a dread. Specific things are smaller.
